reviews.review_text:
I played The Forest for the first time last year. I loved it and also played Sons of the Forest. Even though the second game added more details, improved graphics, and cannibals that act more intelligently with better AI mechanics, I found the gameplay of the first game better and more sustainable, with a smoother learning curve that helps the player get used to the game naturally. Even at the beginning of the game, when I had nothing and was vulnerable, the cannibals were not too aggressive, the first game has a good startup mechanism, while the second game feels more complex. Overall, I like the first game experience more. After I finished both games, I came back to the first game for all the completions. I waited to write this review until I reached 100%.
Positively, The Forest is the best open world survival game that I have ever played. I love the map and how everything is located. You learn about plants and animals in a fun way. There is also a building and crafting aspect that could easily be a separate game on its own. That is very creative. The graphics are also very good. I love the story. It is mysterious and engaging. I learned so much about the cannibals and how to kill or avoid them.
If I have to say one negative thing about the game and this is the only one. It is how the AI was implemented for the cannibals. Okay, the developers wanted the cannibals to move unpredictably, and that is fine, but they were disappearing and appearing 5 meters away in less than a second. I do not think this is a glitch, but somehow it comes from the implementation. I am not sure it was intentional, though.
Anyway, the whole game experience was fun for me. If I could erase my memory, I would want to play it again :D
I saw The Forest 3 in the space mentioned online, and I simply cannot wait for it <3

The Forest as a game is a mystery to me - because I simply can not understand how an unfinished unoriginal game that has nothing impressive in it can go out of early access and still get this much praise.
[list]
    [*] I will start with the most obvious problem: basebuilding. While the game advertises itself with “build a base at day, defend it at night”, the base in question is broken on each and every level. First, building it takes a ridiculous amount of time because of the volume of the required resources. To make things worse - cutting all those trees to make a base makes the local population more aggressive and attacks on your base more often and as a result - instead of being more safe you end up being less safe with a base. The next reason why your base sucks that much is the fact that all of the things you build can be destroyed just in a couple of hits by some monsters or in under a minute by a group of cannibals - and destroyed buildings don’t just drop their materials - they disintegrate forcing you to waste even more time on logging and building. And the last problem with the base makes the whole situation just ridiculous - you don’t even need a base, as enemies are incapable of following you into caves or onto the boat where you can sleep freely and securely.
    [*] Survival elements. I will never stop hating survival systems where the food and water are abundant and extremely easy to get, but the bars run out so fast that you are constantly nagged with hunger and thirst. And just so you understand how easy the food situation is here - you can forage for berries, hunt for wildlife or eat your enemies - and each of those options can sustain you easily, not to mention that you can combine them however you like.
    [*] Combat. Oh god, I haven’t seen that much jank in a while. Sure, on one hand locals behave in an interesting way, with their running, screaming from a distance and everything, but when it comes to a fight having those lunatics run in and out without doing anything while you are incapable of catching up to them becomes annoying extremely fast. It becomes even more annoying when the same exact type of enemies either dies in 2 or 20 hits. It becomes infuriating when said enemies decide to ignore the staggering effect of your attacks and stunlock you with an unending flurry of blows. It could be tolerable if said combat was rare, but sadly you can barely pass a day without some lunatic trying to kill you or ruin your base. Combat just sucks and there is no other way to put it.
   [*] Content for content's sake. This game has a lot of stuff that is simply a waste of time. For example - there is a snow biome and you can waste a lot of time crafting frost protection gear. Sadly the snow biome itself has nothing in it - so all of that collecting was a waste of time.  Another example - there are 4 axes in the game. Even after finishing the game I can’t really tell if they had any difference between them or was it done just to make a list of tools bigger. Basebuilding too falls into that category - there is simply no use or function for it. I assume that at least some of those things are caused by an early release with some ideas gutted - but we will never know for sure.
   [*] Inventory. Sure, having all of the objects visible on a blanket is a neat way of showing what you have - if you are not holding 50 different items. Now imagine that you do - sticks, stones, spears, bows, aforementioned 4 axes, 5 types of hides, a dozen types of berries, plants and herbal mixes - and now try to find anything in this cluttered mess. Yes, with time you do memorize where your things are, at least those that you use often. Finding stuff that you use rarely becomes increasingly annoying the further you go into the game.
   [*] Exploration/spelunking. I get it, there are places even IRL with vast cave systems. I get it, the caves are dark without a proper source of light. What I don’t get at all - why isn't there a proper source of light in your whole god forsaken game? Lighter gives no light and goes off every few seconds. Flashlight is so bad that I could get more light from a single LED. If said caves were some kind of side content - it would be one thing, but it is your main plot route, how come exploring this route is inconvenient? Not to mention that there are a lot of places with nothing in it - like long swimming sections that serve only as a gear check to make sure you got a required item to pass - navigating those is not fun and is, in fact, extremely boring.
   [*] Zero direction. In most other games this would not be a problem. However in this one you are supposed to go through caves in a certain order - the first one has a map and a compass, the next few give you  tools that are required to get through the further caves. Sadly, with zero directions, we often entered caves that we cannot finish without items that we haven’t found at that point, and just as a cherry on top - we found the first cave (the one with the map) as our last cave. If your only chance to get the correct order is to check wiki - sorry, this is a bad game design.
   [*] Plot/world. On paper you have one. But the more you see the more questions you have and the less coherent the story becomes. Ancient artifact from dead space that can resurrect people? Ok, how does it explain the fact that there is an entire tribe of cannibals here? Or the fact that someone hauled hundreds of dead people into the caves? And where did those people even come from, why don’t their bodies decompose? It just feels like different bits were thrown into the game to give it a creepy vibe with a “figure it out yourself” attitude because doing so was much easier than making a believable story and world.
[/list]
Overall, I just can’t find even one thing in this game that was properly made. The whole experience was between boring and frustrating, I don’t think that I would even finish this game if I wasn’t playing with a friend. Don’t bother with this one and just go buy something that doesn’t suck.

I really wanted to enjoy The Forest, but it turned out to be a big letdown. It quickly becomes a repetitive lumberjack simulator filled with endless tree chopping and resource grinding.

The world feels empty, building is frustratingly restrictive, caves are way too dark, and the cannibals become annoying rather than scary. Hunger management is also too punishing early on.

Great idea, poor execution. Disappointed.

This is everything I want in a survival horror game. Base building, cannibal fighting, and chainsaws. Five stars.

I enjoyed the first few hours but then I quickly came to find the gameplay loop a little too annoying to enjoy myself any longer. I don't really understand how this has such good reviews. There were some things i like and disliked about this. 

The Bad:
- The food and energy run out way too quickly and yet despite this there is an overabundance of food and water that is constantly respawning. It just turns it into an annoying tedious thing I have to constantly deal with. 
- Most of my time was spent walking around in caves which are scarry because they are filled with cannibals and eldritch horror monsters but I just can't see a thing while I'm fighting them, so again it is annoying. 
- There isn't really a reason to build bases other than to satisfy your creativity. The enemies just easily destroy your buildings like its nothing and you can actually accidentally hit your own building very easily if you are fighting cannibals near it. 
- I didn't finish the story but what i did play felt unintuitive to progress and not particularly interesting.
- The combat is terrible. it is very difficult to hit enemies with your weapons and basic enemies take something like 20 spear stabs to kill. Despite this, the enemies are laughably easy to kill because you just side strafe all their attacks. 
- you are constantly bombarded by patrols of cannibals so you never get time to rest, explore, and build. 
- Exploration wasn't very rewarding, I swam out to an interesting looking island only be rewarded with absolutely nothing being there.

The Good:
- The crafting system is interesting because there are no crafting recipes, just you jamming a bunch of stuff together until you find out what combines into what. 
- The game definitely has some scary moments.
I've completed this game several times, although every time i enter the cave, i often experience fps drops

A survival crafting game with almost no reason to actually engage in the base-building mechanics. Combat is easy but exceptionally janky (poor hit detection). The story is somewhat interesting but very poorly told. 

This game does not hold up in 2026. There are numerous other games in this genre that do everything better.

How do you make a survival game where players have to go to Reddit to learn how to cook meat? The game does a terrible job explaining basic mechanics.

Also slow as ♥♥♥♥. Progression feels like a chore, not gameplay.

Unintuitive, tedious, and not worth the time.

"The Forest" is honestly one of those survival games that just has something special about it and even today I still consider it one of the best survival experiences from older years. I mainly played it with friends and in my opinion this game was truly made for coop, because the atmosphere becomes absolutely amazing when you are exploring together, building a base, getting lost in caves or suddenly hearing cannibals running somewhere in the forest around you. Playing this game at night creates a completely different level of tension and fear, especially when enemies jump out from bushes or you hear strange noises deep in the caves. The mix of survival and horror here is genuinely fantastic and few games manage to combine those two things this well. What also surprised me is how well this game has aged, because even today it really does not feel outdated when it comes to atmosphere, graphics or gameplay and it still easily competes with many newer survival games. The story is also much more interesting than people might expect at first and slowly discovering what actually happened on the island keeps the game engaging until the end. And honestly the price of this game during sales is absurdly good, it becomes so cheap that it almost feels criminal not to buy it and experience it at least once.

This game has been out for a long time. I'll start by saying that I didn't expect much out of it. I've known of its existence for a while, and was terrified when I played for like 30 minutes as a child. I picked it up with friends, expecting to kill cannibals for fun, and I did! But let me tell you, that's not ALL you'll be doing. Play it, have fun, master the island, and finish it. This game is a must play for any survival/horror fans, and the lore is STRONG. Buy it no questions asked.
One of the classics, from its generation. Must play

I bought this game to relax, chop wood, and build a nice cabin. Instead, I crashed a plane, forgot my son Timmy existed after 20 minutes, and became the main target of a local cannibal gymnastics club that hates interior design.

By hour 8, I was hiding in a bush, covered in mud, eating a suspicious leg of unknown origin, watching a mutant named "Virginia" completely obliterate my stick fence.

Pros: Great graphics, fun building mechanics, excellent cardio simulator.
Cons: The neighbors have zero boundaries and will dive-bomb through your roof at 3 AM.

If you love paranoia and screaming with your friends on Discord at 2 AM, buy this game.

The Ultimate Co-op Survival Horror Experience!
After spending over 27 hours surviving the wilderness and looking for Timmy, I can confidently say this game offers the perfect blend of spine-chilling horror and pure co-op fun.

Unmatched Multiplayer Fun: This game masterfully transitions from a terrifying horror experience into a chaotic, action-packed comedy when played with friends. Chopping down trees together, sharing resources, and reviving each other in a pinch makes multiplayer unforgettable.

Engaging Survival & Base Building: Building massive fortresses, setting up defensive traps, and hunting for food feels incredibly satisfying when you divide the tasks among your squad.

Terrifying Caves and Cannibal AI: Exploring the pitch-black cave systems will genuinely test your nerves. The eerie shrieks of cannibals and mutants echoing through the night never fail to trigger absolute panic among the group.

Easily one of the best co-op survival games ever made. Get your friends, build a base, and get ready for a perfect mix of jumpscares and laughter! 9.5/10.
One of the best game to play with friends, a lot of fun and screams...
i just completed the forest an its my first open world and story game and i really recomend it. Its a really good game and a great story and for only 19 euro u shuld play it
Every time I try to load a save or join my friend on an existing save in multiplayer, the game gets stuck loading.
Even though I already 100%-ed this game twice before, and finished the main story more than 10 times, it was still a cathartic experience to play it through again.

The Forest is a survival game I truly enjoy, especially for its multiplayer mode and the tension of staying alive together. The mechanics of building, crafting, and cooperation create a strong sense of engagement, though one aspect feels less appealing: enemies sometimes appear suddenly without clear reason, chasing the player without context. This unpredictability can break immersion, even though the core survival and teamwork elements remain engaging. For those who appreciate survival challenges with friends, the game still delivers a memorable experience.

[h1]Rating: 7/10 -Decent[/h1]

[h1]TLDR:[/h1]
[b]A decent survival game that does not take you by the hand.[/b]


This is the game that kicked off the indie open world survival craft genre hype back in the 2010's IMO.


[h1]Positive:[/h1]

[b]Story:[/b]
The game is not story driven. You crash land on a peninsula. Your son gets kidnapped and your goal is to find him. That's about it. You're free to do whatever you want. You will stumble across story elements while exploring the world. The game doesn't hold your hand and won't to tell you what to do. I was convinced that I'm going to need a guide to finish the game. I managed to finish it without one. Exploring the caves will progress the story naturally. You just have to stay curious and explore the world. I was really impressed that I managed to find all the story relevant items on my own. However, realizing that only the caves provide story progression made me feel like the overworld is obsolete. You can finish the game in like half an hour if you know where to find all the story items and good gear.

[b]World:[/b]
The peninsula is a big forest. It feels untouched by humanity. It feels mysterious and I think I'm being watched. There are cannibals walking amongst the trees. You can cut down trees to create some space for yourself but the cannibals will notice your presence. Navigating the forests in the dark it's quite scary.

[b]Caves:[/b]
The highlight of the game. They are scary and dark. You walk and explore in the deep, dark and wet depths. You can find all sorts of loot and enemies down there. You start to question: "how deep does this go?". The caves can feel endless but they will end eventually. 

[b]Crafting:[/b]
I like it and hate it at the same time. It's quite simple. Your inventory is a big mat where your items get displayed. Putting things into the centre of the mat will let you combine those materials. Putting items into the centre and hovering over them with your cursor will also reveal possible crafting recipes. Something that I don't like about the system is that your mat will become quite stacked the longer you play and you will start to struggle to find things in your pile of items.


[h1]Negative:[/h1]

[b]Performance:[/b]
Doesn’t perform very well. Even on a modern system (my specs are down below). I‘ve got 100-150 average FPS (which for some reason don't look like 100+ FPS) on max settings and 1440p which is more than enough to play. However, a game that looks this bad should run better. It should run at 300 FPS. The graphical fidelity is abysmal. Low poly models that all have a weirdly wet looking shiny glow, low res textures and simplistic map geometry shouldn’t use this much processing power. The frames can start to drop to 60 FPS if 5 or more cannibals are on screen. This game looks like it could run on a phone yet struggles to perform on decent hardware.

[b]Sanity Meter:[/b]
The game has a sanity meter that increases and decreases. Eating human flesh will decrease it while sleeping and eating proper food will increase it. There is not much too it. You don’t get any penalties when it’s low. You unlock the ability to build effigies though when it gets below 90%. They are built using human body parts and the will scare off cannibals when lit on fire. They could have done some interesting stuff with it like starting to hear voices or see things that aren’t there. Having a nightmare with jumpscare when going to bed. Something like that. The mechanic is literally unfinished and can be ignored.


[h1]Conclusion:[/h1]

It's a decent game that is lacking some polish. The game was a cool experience though. Exploring the caves and in finding good loot was fun. I recommend the game.

The Forest is a mixed bag. Despite its age, the game looks and runs beautifully. However, I can't say the same for technicality and gameplay. Building a base is pretty much pointless, combat is boring and list of things to do in the game is quite narrow. As for technical problems, I can't drink water from a stream or fill my waterskin. Why? Because it wasn't defined as a water source to begin with in code. I will say it is a creative game, which excels at specific things for 2014-2015 standards, but highly overrated.

EVERY PERSON HAS TO SAVE!!!! IF YOU PLAY MULTIPLAYER, EVERY PERSON HAS TO SAVE. My friend and I just played 3.5 hours, we were under the impression that if the host (me) saves, then it saves the progress of everyone else. That is NOT THE CASE. EVERY SINGLE PERSON HAS TO SAVE. I wish they had that as a warning or put that somewhere in the game, but it's literally not there.

Besides that, it was really fun playing. I haven't finished playing it yet, but for the time that my friend and I spent playing, I felt like it was really worth it. I highly recommend it. Just make sure to save.

-mini heart attack
-alt + f4

Never played solo after that

Looking back, The Forest remains one of the most defining multiplayer experiences of its era. Years after leaving that map, the memories forged with friends in its suffocating woods still linger. You do not need to follow the narrative path to feel the weight of this game; the true story is the one you write with your squad while trying to survive the night.

Overall, this game is an absolute blast and I highly recommend it. Whether you are hunting down the story or just building a base with your mates, it is a flawless multiplayer experience. 10/10

🌲 🪵 The Forest – honestly, this is a mandatory, absolute must-play. It is one of the biggest and most hyped survival games of all time for a reason. The only survival game that even comes close to beating it is Minecraft, but let’s be real, Minecraft is way more of a creative sandbox than a gritty, terrifying fight for survival.

If you have never played The Forest in your entire life... MIJOOO, what are you actually doing? The game is a total banger, and the absolute best way to experience it is co-op with friends. Getting lost in dark caves, building a base together, and screaming when a group of mutants jumps you out of nowhere is unmatched entertainment.

I will admit, after about 40 hours or so, the gameplay loop can start to feel a bit repetitive and lose its steam once the initial terror wears off. But the journey to get to that point is incredible. Plus, Endnight proved they are top-tier developers, taking everything that made this game a classic and upgrading it with Sons of the Forest.

If you haven’t experienced this masterpiece yet, do yourself a favor: either go buy it right now or just sell your computer.

🟢 Pure co-op chaos that makes for unforgettable, terrifying gaming nights with friends
🌲 A genuinely creepy atmosphere that delivers better survival mechanics than almost anything else on the market
🔴 The late-game can start to feel a bit boring once you've conquered the map around the 40-hour mark

8/10 – "If you haven't played this yet, just sell your PC."
